content.problemStatement:
Chef has 222 strings AAA and BBB of equal length NNN. Both strings contain lowercase english alphabets only.

Chef can perform several moves on string AAA. In one move, Chef has to:


Select an index i (1≤i≤N−1)i \space (1 \leq i \leq N-1)i (1≤i≤N−1).
Replace A[i]A[i]A[i] with (A[i]+1)(A[i]+1)(A[i]+1).
Replace A[i+1]A[i+1]A[i+1] with (A[i+1]+1)(A[i+1]+1)(A[i+1]+1).
For example, if A=abczeA = \texttt{abcze}A=abcze, a valid move would be to select index 333. This way the string becomes abdae\texttt{abdae}abdae after performing the move. Note that the value at an index is cyclically incremented. This means that, a→b\texttt{a} \rightarrow \texttt{b}a→b, b→c\texttt{b} \rightarrow \texttt{c}b→c, …\ldots…, z→a\texttt{z} \rightarrow \texttt{a}z→a.

Chef has been asked to answer QQQ queries. Each query is of the form:


LLL RRR: Given 1≤L≤R≤N1 \leq L \leq R \leq N1≤L≤R≤N, Chef wants to know if it is possible to convert the substring A[L,R]A[L, R]A[L,R] to B[L,R]B[L, R]B[L,R] by performing the above mentioned move **any** number of times.
For each query, output in a single line Yes\texttt{Yes}Yes, if the conversion is possible using any number of moves. Otherwise, print No\texttt{No}No.

**NOTE:** Queries are **independent**. Thus, the original strings AAA and BBB would be retained to process next query. For each query solve for substrings A[L,R]A[L,R]A[L,R] and B[L,R]B[L,R]B[L,R] only. So valid values for iii will be among LLL to R−1R - 1R−1 only.


Input Format

The first line will contain TTT - the number of test cases. Then the test cases follow.
First line of each test case contains two integers N,QN, QN,Q.
Second line of each test case contains string AAA.
Third line of each test case contains string BBB.
QQQ lines follow, where the ithi^{th}ith line contains two integers LiRiL_i R_iLi​Ri​ - the ithi^{th}ith query.


Output Format
Output QQQ lines, where the ithi^{th}ith line contains the answer to the ithi^{th}ith query. The answer is Yes\texttt{Yes}Yes, if the conversion is possible using any number of moves. Otherwise, the answer is No\texttt{No}No.


You may print each character of the string in uppercase or lowercase (for example, the strings yEs\texttt{yEs}yEs, yes\texttt{yes}yes, Yes\texttt{Yes}Yes and YES\texttt{YES}YES will all be treated as identical).
constraints: Sum of NNN over all test cases does not exceed 2⋅1052 \cdot 10^52⋅105.
Sum of QQQ over all test cases does not exceed 2⋅1052 \cdot 10^52⋅105.

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Explanation:
Test Case 111:

For the first query, the substring A[2,2]=bA[2,2] = \texttt{b}A[2,2]=b is already same as B[2,2]=bB[2,2] = \texttt{b}B[2,2]=b. Thus, the answer is Yes\texttt{Yes}Yes.
For the second query, it can be proven that the substring A[1,3]A[1,3]A[1,3] can never be made equal to B[1,3]B[1,3]B[1,3] using any number of moves.
For the third query, the substring A[4,5]=dzA[4,5] = \texttt{dz}A[4,5]=dz and B[4,5]=eaB[4,5] = \texttt{ea}B[4,5]=ea. Applying the move on index 444, we get A[4]=eA[4] = \texttt{e}A[4]=e and A[5]=aA[5] = \texttt{a}A[5]=a. Hence, after one move, A[4,5]=B[4,5]A[4,5] = B[4,5]A[4,5]=B[4,5]. Thus, the answer is Yes\texttt{Yes}Yes in this case.
